metadata.title: Introduction to Investment Types
metadata.subtitle: Understanding Stocks, Bonds, Mutual Funds, and ETFs to Build Long-Term Wealth

content.title: Introduction to Investment Types
content.subtitle: Understanding Stocks, Bonds, Mutual Funds, and ETFs to Build Long-Term Wealth

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Priya, 24, landed her first full-time job earning $45,000 annually. Feeling overwhelmed by investment choices, she researched simple strategies.

She decided to invest 15% of her income ($563/month) split between two low-cost index ETFs: 80% stocks, 20% bonds—both with expense ratios under 0.05%.

By age 30, her disciplined approach built a portfolio worth over $52,000 despite market volatility—all from a simple two-fund strategy.

How can understanding different investment types help you build wealth aligned with your financial goals and risk tolerance?

objectives.description: Analyze the characteristics and functions of major investment types including stocks, bonds, mutual funds, and ETFs.
Compare the risk-return profiles of different investment vehicles and how they align with financial goals.
Evaluate how different investment types fit various time horizons—from short-term goals to retirement.
Calculate potential returns and assess the impact of fees on long-term wealth accumulation.

terms.definition: Ownership shares in a company that entitle you to a portion of the company's assets and earnings.
terms.example: Owning 100 shares of Apple means you own a tiny piece of the company and may receive dividends.

terms.definition: A loan you make to a government or corporation that pays regular interest and returns your principal at maturity.
terms.example: A $1,000 10-year Treasury bond paying 4% gives you $40/year in interest, then returns your $1,000.

terms.definition: An investment vehicle that pools money from many investors to purchase a diversified portfolio of stocks, bonds, or other securities.
terms.example: A growth mutual fund might hold 100+ stocks, giving you instant diversification with one purchase.

terms.definition: Similar to a mutual fund but trades on exchanges like stocks; typically has lower fees and often tracks a market index.
terms.example: A total stock market ETF with 0.03% fees gives you exposure to thousands of companies for pennies.

leftPanel.title: The Power of Market Returns
paragraphs: Historically, the S&P 500 has averaged approximately 10% annual returns—but with substantial year-to-year volatility.
This means money invested in the stock market has historically doubled roughly every 7-8 years.
The key is time in the market, not timing the market. Consistent investing over decades builds serious wealth.

highlightBox.text: $500/month invested at 8% for 30 years becomes over $700,000—from only $180,000 in contributions.

stats.label: Growth from compound interest over 30 years

leftColumn.title: Stocks (Ownership)
items: You become a part-owner of the company
Higher volatility—can lose 30%+ in bad years
Growth comes from price appreciation + dividends

rightColumn.title: Bonds (Lending)
items: You lend money to a borrower
Best for stability or near-term goals
Returns come from regular interest payments

leftColumn.title: Mutual Funds
items: Trades once daily at closing price

rightColumn.title: ETFs
items: Trades throughout the day like stocks
Buy as little as one share

layoutData.title: "Don't Put All Your Eggs in One Basket"
paragraphs: Diversification spreads investments across different asset types, industries, and securities to reduce risk.
bulletPoints: Why it works: When one investment falls, others may rise or stay stable, smoothing overall returns.
Single stock risk: One company can go bankrupt (Enron, Lehman Brothers). An index fund holds thousands.
Asset class diversification: Stocks and bonds often move in opposite directions—when stocks crash, bonds often rise.
Geographic diversification: US, international, and emerging markets don't always move together.
The "free lunch": Diversification can reduce risk without reducing expected returns—rare in investing.

keyPoint.text: 🎯 A total stock market index fund gives you instant diversification across thousands of companies for less than 0.10% in fees.

leftPanel.title: Small Percentages, Massive Impact
paragraphs: Fees compound against you just like returns compound for you.
Example: $500/month for 30 years at 8%:
• 0.03% fee (index ETF): $733,904
• 0.75% fee (avg mutual fund): $654,165
• 1.25% fee (high-cost fund): $600,430
That 1.22% fee difference costs you $133,474—over 22 years of contributions!

highlightBox.text: Always check expense ratios before investing. A 1% fee difference can cost you hundreds of thousands.

stats.label: Cost of 1.22% higher fees over 30 years

layoutData.title: Your Time Horizon Determines Your Risk Capacity
paragraphs: The amount of time before you need your money determines how much volatility you can tolerate:
bulletPoints: 40+ years (retirement in your 20s): 90% stocks / 10% bonds—aggressive growth. You have decades to recover from downturns.
20-30 years (mid-career retirement): 80% stocks / 20% bonds—growth-focused. Still plenty of time for compounding.
10-20 years (approaching retirement): 60% stocks / 40% bonds—balanced. Begin protecting accumulated wealth.
5-10 years (near-term goal): 40% stocks / 60% bonds—conservative. Reduce volatility as goal approaches.
Under 5 years (short-term goal): 20% stocks / 80% bonds or cash—preservation. Can't afford a market crash.

keyPoint.text: 📅 The younger you are, the more stocks you can hold. As you age, gradually shift toward bonds.

leftPanel.title: Evidence-Based Approaches
paragraphs: The Three-Fund Portfolio:
1. Total US Stock Market Index
2. Total International Stock Index
3. Total US Bond Market Index
This simple approach gives you global diversification with minimal effort and fees under 0.10%.
Target-Date Funds:
Pick your retirement year, invest, done. The fund automatically shifts from stocks to bonds as you age.

scenario.name: Alex's Retirement Start
paragraphs: Alex, 22, just started his first job. He's enrolled in his company's 401(k) with a 4% employer match.
He's choosing between a target-date 2065 fund (0.15% fee) or selecting individual funds himself.

outcomes.label: Best Move
outcomes.detail: Get the full employer match (free money!)

scenario.name: Jordan's House Fund
paragraphs: Jordan, 28, is saving for a house down payment. She wants to buy in 3 years and has $15,000 saved.
She's wondering whether to invest it in stocks for potential growth or keep it somewhere safer.

outcomes.label: Stock Risk
outcomes.detail: A crash could delay purchase by years

outcomes.label: Key Principle
outcomes.detail: 3 years is too short for stock volatility

content.question:
Why do financial advisors typically recommend younger investors hold more stocks and older investors hold more bonds?

How does your time horizon affect your ability to recover from market downturns?

At what point would you start shifting from stocks to bonds?

Diversification is often called "the only free lunch in investing."

How does spreading investments across different asset types, industries, and geographies reduce risk without reducing expected returns?

Why is a single stock riskier than an index fund holding thousands of stocks?

questions.question: Which investment type typically offers the highest long-term returns but with greater volatility: stocks, bonds, or CDs?
What is the primary advantage of ETFs over traditional mutual funds?
A 1% fee difference over 30 years on $500/month investments costs approximately how much?
Why should someone saving for a goal 3 years away avoid investing heavily in stocks?

takeaways.title: Stocks for Growth, Bonds for Stability
takeaways.description: Stocks offer higher returns with more volatility; bonds provide stability and income. Mix based on your timeline.

takeaways.title: ETFs Beat High-Fee Funds
takeaways.description: Low-cost index ETFs outperform most actively managed funds over time—largely because of lower fees.

takeaways.title: Diversify to Reduce Risk
takeaways.description: Spreading investments across asset types, industries, and geographies reduces risk without reducing expected returns.

takeaways.title: Match Time Horizon to Risk
takeaways.description: Longer time horizons allow more stock exposure; shorter timelines require more conservative investments.

main.title: Build Your First Portfolio
main.description: You've learned about investment types—now design a portfolio that matches YOUR goals and timeline.

steps: Define your investment goal and time horizon
Research 2-3 low-cost index ETFs that fit your allocation
Compare expense ratios and calculate 30-year fee impact
Create your investment plan with specific fund selections

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
